[JS Weird World] No.62 Reading the jQuery Source Code to Learn JavaScript

In this one we're going to use jQuery to learn JavaScript — look at how it's structured and how to use it. We're not trying to understand how every single feature is built. The real goal is to see whether we can read actual source code and learn from how it's put together.

Whether or not you've ever written jQuery, I'd bet that by the time you're reading this chapter you've at least heard of it. But don't worry if you haven't. jQuery is just a library on top of JavaScript. Its whole selling point is that it simplifies a ton of syntax — it lets us type less to get the same thing done, and it helps smooth over cross-browser issues (Firefox, Safari, Google Chrome, Internet Explorer).
Enough talk, let's follow along with the course example.

var q = $('ul.people li'); // this is how we select the ul li
// this is how we select the ul li
A jQuery object containing the selected li elements
You can see we got back an object, and each
is an element — also known as a DOM element.
And when we try to peek at the prototype, we can see a whole bunch of methods sitting on it.
Many methods sitting on the jQuery prototype
Like we covered in earlier chapters, putting methods on the prototype is the better move — it's better for performance.
See the Pen 深入瞭解原始碼：jQeury（一） by HF (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
Now let's try to get a feel for how jQuery works under the hood. (Heads up: this is going to look pretty different from the course example, because the version I'm referencing is 3.4.1.)
Opening up the jQuery source code

Obviously we're not going to read this line by line. We just pick out the parts we already recognize and absorb those. Before that though, I'd suggest opening the source yourself and skimming it — the point is to not let it scare you. Try to find the bits you can actually understand. For example, see if you can spot an IIFE in there.
Turns out there's an IIFE right near the top.
An IIFE near the top of the jQuery source
A typeof check in the source
This part up here is checking our environment.
And this line is jQuery checking whether
is the global object in our environment.
jQuery checking whether window is the global object
There's one thing in here that really matters — this
Scroll down a little more and you'll see a pile of features, but they all share one thing in common: they're all written inside an object, using the object-override style.
The part after this gets pretty complicated, so I'd honestly recommend going straight to the course explanation for that.
